body: On the product detail page, inspect whether stock availability status is clearly communicated — the page should explicitly indicate whether the product (and each selected variant, if applicable) is in stock, temporarily out of stock, or discontinued, and should provide a constructive path forward for unavailable items rather than a dead-end 'Out of Stock' message. Positive signals include a clear in-stock indicator near the Add-to-Cart button (e.g., 'In Stock', 'Ships in 2–3 days'), a 'Notify Me When Available' or 'Back-Order' option for temporarily unavailable items, visible alternative product recommendations on the same page for out-of-stock items, and a 'Discontinued' or 'No Longer Available' label for permanently delisted products. Negative signals include no stock status indicator at all, an out-of-stock item page that only shows a disabled Add-to-Cart button without explanation, requiring account creation to access a back-in-stock notification option, or failing to surface alternative products when the primary item is unavailable — all patterns that create a dead-end browsing experience that drives users to competitors. When violations are found, recommend displaying explicit stock status adjacent to the Add-to-Cart button, surfacing 3–5 alternative product recommendations below the fold for out-of-stock items, and enabling back-in-stock notification without requiring account creation. Baymard Institute's research on out-of-stock product handling found that 68% of sites do not allow users to purchase temporarily out-of-stock products, and that 30% of users are likely to abandon the site to look for the product elsewhere when no purchase or notification option is provided.

provenance.citation_text: 68% of sites don't allow users to place orders for temporarily out of stock products. This limitation creates a problematic user experience. The research reveals that 30% are likely to simply abandon to look for the product elsewhere when encountering an out-of-stock product with no purchase option. The research distinguishes between two scenarios. For temporarily unavailable items, sites should allow users to purchase the products and simply increase the delivery time, while also displaying alternatives. For discontinued products, sites must prominently display text such as 'Discontinued' or 'Deprecated' at the top of the product page. The study also notes that alternative notification strategies perform poorly. When sites rely on 'Email me when in stock' buttons or wishlist features requiring account creation, users typically abandon these options. The data indicates 75% of sites require users to create an account to save products, which further discourages engagement.

ai_review.reviewer_persona: neural-heuristic-reviewer v1.0 (top-1% senior CRO consultant; 20yr; multi-vertical D2C/SaaS/marketplace/B2B/lead-gen/fintech/media)
ai_review.why_generated: Drafter targeted Baymard's OOS-handling research (68% sites blocking OOS purchase paths instead of providing alternatives/restock-notify) — high-impact Baymard PDP finding.
ai_review.how_reviewed: URL matches Baymard handling-out-of-stock research (HTTP 200); 68% blocking-rate consistent with Baymard documented benchmark; restock-notify + alternatives recommendation is canonical Baymard pattern; weight=0.70 appropriate for revenue-recovery heuristic.

dimension_findings.finding: URL matches Baymard /blog/handling-out-of-stock-products pattern; HTTP 200 verified
68% OOS-blocking stat is documented Baymard PDP-stock benchmark
pdp-only limited; D2C+marketplace appropriate; physical-stock-bearing products
no conversion-rate predictions; phrasing is observational/recommendation-form
